metadata.summary: Django models design: BaseModel inheritance, SoftDelete, TextChoices, database constraints, ForeignKey on_delete, custom QuerySets, indexes, N+1 prevention.

content.title: Django Models
content.summary: Django models are thin data containers: fields, indexes, constraints, and __str__. Business logic belongs in services (HackSoft style). Every domain model inherits from BaseModel (uid + timestamps). Status fields use TextChoices/IntegerChoices. ForeignKey on_delete is chosen deliberately — PROTECT for user/account refs, CASCADE only for child rows with no meaning without parent. Every generated migration file is reviewed by a human before migrate.

why.p: Thin models and a service layer keep business rules testable, migrations predictable, and ORM queries composable via custom QuerySets. Models bloated with business logic become impossible to test without a database and export their logic to views as copy-paste code.

Omitting full_clean() before save() in services silently drops model-level validation. Omitting related_name generates confusing model_set reverse names that break when models are renamed. Every db_index=True added without justification bloats write amplification. CASCADE on_delete for user references causes irreversible data loss when accounts are deleted — a common production incident.

list.item: Adding a new model to a Django app.
Refactoring a model bloated with business logic into thin model + service functions.
Audit: scanning for missing db_index, wrong on_delete, missing Meta.constraints.
Designing status/state fields — converting raw string choices to TextChoices.
Migrating Django 4.x → 5.x to use db_default and GeneratedField.
Code review of Django model definitions for design consistency and best practices.
ORM-less projects (FastAPI + SQLAlchemy, Flask + SQLAlchemy) — route to SQLAlchemy methodology.
Migrating to a different ORM (peewee, Tortoise, SQLModel) — conventions don't translate.
Quick throwaway prototypes where Django Admin + auto-generated tables are sufficient.
Reporting/read-only projects backed by views or external warehouses.

rule.statement: Every domain model inherits from BaseModel (uid + created_at + updated_at). Never inherit from models.Model directly for domain entities.
rule.rationale: Consistent base fields across all models enables audit trails and safe public APIs without per-model boilerplate.

example.title: Model using BaseModel with uid and timestamps

rule.statement: Models must be thin: fields, Meta, and __str__ only. Business logic belongs in services/. Always call full_clean() before save() in service functions.
rule.rationale: full_clean() is not called automatically by save(). Omitting it silently bypasses model-level validation (clean(), validators, constraints).

example.title: Service layer handling business logic and validation
example.code: # apps/users/services.py def user_create(*, email: str, name: str, password: str) -> User: user = User(email=email, name=name) user.set_password(password) user.full_clean() # must be explicit — save() does not call it user.save() return user

example.title: Business logic in model methods — hard to test

rule.statement: Always use TextChoices or IntegerChoices for enumerated fields. Never use raw string tuples as choices.
rule.rationale: TextChoices provides .choices, .values, .labels, and .names; raw tuples require parallel string maintenance and are caught by ruff DJ rules.

example.title: Using TextChoices for status fields
example.code:
# apps/orders/constants.py from django.db import models
class OrderStatus(models.TextChoices): PENDING = 'pending', 'Pending' PROCESSING = 'processing', 'Processing' SHIPPED = 'shipped', 'Shipped' CANCELLED = 'cancelled', 'Cancelled'
# apps/orders/models.py class Order(BaseModel): status = models.CharField( max_length=20, choices=OrderStatus.choices, default=OrderStatus.PENDING, )

rule.statement: Use Meta.constraints for invariants that span multiple fields. Use CheckConstraint for value-range rules and UniqueConstraint for conditional uniqueness.
rule.rationale: DB-level constraints enforce invariants even when ORM validation is bypassed (bulk_create, raw SQL). CheckConstraint with condition requires PostgreSQL — pin the backend in a comment.

example.title: Database constraints for data integrity

section.name: foreignkey-on-delete

rule.statement: Choose on_delete deliberately: PROTECT for user/account refs (delete is an event, not silent cascade), CASCADE only for child rows with no meaning without parent, SET_NULL for optional assignees.
rule.rationale: CASCADE on user references causes irreversible data loss when an account is deleted — a common production incident.

section.p: Relationship Types: User/Account reference = PROTECT (deletion is an event). Child rows (e.g. OrderItem under Order) = CASCADE (child has no meaning without parent). Optional assignee (e.g. assigned_to) = SET_NULL (row survives reassignment). Audit trail reference = PROTECT (preserve historical record).

rule.statement: Define domain-specific filter logic as QuerySet methods, not in service functions or views. Use Manager.from_queryset(QuerySet)() — note the trailing () to instantiate.
rule.rationale: QuerySet methods are chainable; service-level filtering is not. Missing () on from_queryset creates the class object, not an instance — a confusing runtime error.

example.title: Custom QuerySet with chainable methods
SoftDelete QuerySet implementation

rule.statement: If a child model redefines objects, it must replicate the alive() filter or the soft-delete protection is lost.
rule.rationale: Custom managers override the default; naively replacing objects loses the alive filter and returns deleted rows.

rule.statement: Add db_index or a composite Index for every field combination used in filter()/order_by()/distinct() paths that hit production traffic. Require justification — every index costs write amplification.
rule.rationale: Missing indexes cause full-table scans at scale. Unnecessary indexes slow writes and bloat storage.

rule.statement: Use partial indexes (condition=Q(...)) only on PostgreSQL. Comment the backend requirement next to the index definition.
rule.rationale: Partial indexes use PostgreSQL-specific SQL. MySQL and SQLite ignore the condition silently or error at migration time.

example.title: Index strategy balancing reads and writes
example.code: class Meta: indexes = [ models.Index(fields=['user', 'status']), models.Index(fields=['status', 'created_at']), # PostgreSQL only — partial index on pending orders models.Index( fields=['created_at'], condition=Q(status='pending'), name='pending_orders_idx', ), # Covering index — avoids table lookup for summary queries models.Index( fields=['user', 'status'], include=['total'], name='order_summary_idx', ), ]

rule.statement: Use select_related for ForeignKey/OneToOne (JOIN), prefetch_related for reverse FK and ManyToMany (second query). Add these at the QuerySet/Manager level for the common access pattern.
rule.rationale: Per-call select_related is easy to forget; manager-level defaults ensure the optimization is always applied for the most common query shape.

example.title: N+1 query antipattern
Optimized query with select_related

rule.statement: Define cross-field invariants in clean(). Define field-level constraints with validators=[...] on the field. Both require full_clean() to be called explicitly from services.
rule.rationale: DRF and Ninja do not call clean() automatically. Validation placed only in clean() is invisible to API consumers unless services call full_clean().

rule.statement: Use db_default for values the database computes at INSERT time (avoids Python-side time skew). Use GeneratedField for columns computed from other columns. Pin PostgreSQL when using GeneratedField(db_persist=True).
rule.rationale: db_default uses DB NOW() — strictly monotonic in distributed systems. GeneratedField(db_persist=False) is a virtual column unsupported on SQLite.

antipattern.description: Inheriting from models.Model directly instead of the project BaseModel.
antipattern.reason: Breaks uid/timestamps consistency across the codebase. All domain models must use BaseModel. Audit with: grep -r "models.Model" apps/*/models.py and flag direct inheritance.

antipattern.description: Omitting full_clean() in service functions before save().
antipattern.reason: model.save() does not call full_clean(). Validation defined in clean() or field validators is silently skipped.

antipattern.description: Exposing id (PK) and uid both in DRF serializers.
antipattern.reason: Defeats the purpose of UUID. Serializers must exclude id; use uid only. DRF viewsets need lookup_field = 'uid'.

antipattern.description: Using GeneratedField(db_persist=True) or partial indexes on non-PostgreSQL databases.
antipattern.reason: SQLite and MySQL do not fully support these features. Migrations may succeed but fail at query time. Pin the DB engine in a comment next to the field/index.

antipattern.description: Auto-squashing migrations without reviewing the generated migration file.
antipattern.reason: Squash can drop UUID unique=True constraints and re-add without backfilling, corrupting production data. Every migration file must be reviewed by a human before migrate runs.

antipattern.description: Adding db_index=True on every field "for performance" without justification.
antipattern.reason: Every index increases write overhead and storage. Only index fields actually used in filter/order_by/distinct on production traffic.

antipattern.description: Reordering fields in a model "to clean it up".
antipattern.reason: Django generates spurious AlterField migrations on field reorder. Lock field order via code review.

rule.statement: Do not use SoftDeleteModel for models containing personally identifiable information (PII). PII subject to deletion requests requires hard delete.
rule.rationale: Soft delete preserves the data — directly contradicting GDPR Article 17 (right to erasure) deletion requests.
